task: I’ve got a couple of household things I could take care of this week, but I don’t want to rush into the wrong fix. Could you help me sort out the sensible order to handle them and what I should think through before I start anything?

turns.agent_response:
Yes — the easiest way is to sort them by **risk first, then inconvenience**.

## Sensible order
### 1) Anything that could cause damage or be unsafe
- leaks
- electrical problems
- gas or heat issues
- anything with burning smell, sparks, or repeated breaker trips

### 2) Things that affect basic comfort or daily use
- heat
- water
- fridge
- stove
- doors/windows that won’t close right

### 3) Small fixes
- loose hardware
- filters
- minor drips
- simple cleaning or tightening jobs

## Before you start, think through
- **What exactly is wrong?**
- **Is it safe to touch?**
- **Do I know the part or system involved?**
- **What happens if I leave it alone for a week?**
- **Do I have the right tools and replacement parts?**
- **Could this turn into a bigger repair if I guess wrong?**

## Quick rule
If it could damage the house, stop and assess first.  
If it’s just annoying and simple, handle it next.  
If you’re not sure what part you’re looking at, leave it alone until you do.

## Good things to have ready
- flashlight
- screwdriver
- adjustable wrench
- rag or paper towels
- note pad or phone note
- any model numbers or part numbers

If you want, I can turn this into a **simple do-now / check-first / leave-alone list**.

user_simulator.text:
Pretty much the right idea. But I’d want it a little more exact before I start poking at anything.

If it’s me, I’d sort it like this:
- water leak first
- heat or electrical next
- then anything that stops a door, appliance, or fixture from working right
- last, the small nuisance stuff

Before I touch it, I’d want to know:
- is it getting worse, or just staying the same
- can I shut it off or isolate it
- do I already have the right part, or am I guessing
- if I make it worse, what’s the backup plan

That part about breaker trips, burning smell, gas smell — that’s the kind of thing I’d stop on. No sense being brave about it.

If you want to make this useful, give me the actual two or three things you’re looking at. Then I can tell you which one I’d do first and which one I’d leave alone for now.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s the plain order I’d use.

## Do first
- **Water leak**
- **Heat or electrical issue**
- **Anything that stops a door, appliance, or fixture from working right**
- **Small nuisance stuff last**

## Before you touch anything, check:
- Is it **getting worse** or staying the same?
- Can I **shut it off or isolate it**?
- Do I already have the **right part**, or am I guessing?
- If I make it worse, what’s the **backup plan**?

## Stop and leave it alone if you have:
- breaker trips more than once
- burning smell
- gas smell
- sparks
- heat where it shouldn’t be

## Simple rule
If it can damage the house, handle that first.  
If it affects heat or power, handle that next.  
If it’s a door, appliance, or fixture that just isn’t working right, do that after the urgent stuff.  
Small nuisance fixes come last.

If you want, send me the **2 or 3 things** you’re looking at, and I’ll help you sort which one to tackle first.

user_simulator.text:
That’s close enough for a general rule, but I’d want it tied to the actual jobs before I start. The order matters some, but so does what’s behind the problem.

For me, I’d look at:
- whether it involves water getting into walls, floor, or wiring
- whether heat or power is still working normally
- whether I can shut it off cleanly at a valve, breaker, or switch
- whether the part is something I can replace from a known number, not a guess
- what happens if I leave it until tomorrow

And I’d still keep the stop list simple: repeated breaker trips, burning smell, gas smell, sparks, or anything hot where it ought not be. That’s not the time to test your luck.

If you want this to be worth much, name the actual two or three things. Otherwise it stays a little too general for me to trust before I start messing with anything.

turns.agent_response:
Yes — that’s the right way to think about it.

## Use this order
1. **Water getting into walls, floor, or wiring**
2. **Heat or power not working normally**
3. **Can I shut it off cleanly?**
4. **Is the part known, or am I guessing?**
5. **What happens if I wait until tomorrow?**

## Stop list
- repeated breaker trips
- burning smell
- gas smell
- sparks
- anything hot where it shouldn’t be

## Simple rule
- If it can **damage the house**, do that first.
- If it affects **heat or power**, do that next.
- If you can **shut it off cleanly** and know the part, it may be a reasonable fix.
- If you’d be guessing, wait.

If you want, send me the **actual two or three jobs** and I’ll sort them in order for you.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, that’s the shape of it. Still a little broad, though.

What I’d want next is the actual jobs, one by one, with the plain details:
- what it is
- what it’s doing
- where it’s happening
- whether I can shut it off
- and whether there’s any water, heat, or power involved

If you give me that, I can tell you which one comes first and which one waits. Right now it’s still general enough to be useful as a rule, but not enough to make a decision on the bench.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — send me the actual jobs one by one, and I’ll sort them in order.

For each one, just give me:
- **what it is**
- **what it’s doing**
- **where it’s happening**
- **whether you can shut it off**
- **whether water, heat, or power is involved**

Then I’ll tell you:
- which one I’d do first
- which one can wait
- and which ones I’d leave alone

If you want, you can list them in rough form and I’ll clean them up.

user_simulator.text:
A couple things, then.

1) Kitchen sink has a slow drip under the cabinet. Not spraying, just wet at the bottom if I leave a pan under it overnight. I can shut off the valves at the sink.

2) Back bedroom outlet quit working after I plugged in a lamp. No smell, no sparks, but the lamp won’t come on and that outlet is dead. Breaker doesn’t look tripped, but I haven’t gone around resetting anything yet.

3) Bathroom door latch is sticking. Door still closes, just has to be pulled a little hard.

If I were doing them, the leak comes first, then the outlet, then the door latch. The leak is the one I don’t want sitting there. The outlet I’d want to be sure about before I start poking at it, because I don’t like guessing with power. The latch can wait.

turns.agent_response:
That order makes sense.

## I’d do them in this order
1. **Kitchen sink drip**
2. **Back bedroom outlet**
3. **Bathroom door latch**

## Why
- **Kitchen sink drip:** water under a cabinet can turn into wood damage or mold if it sits
- **Outlet:** not urgent if there’s no smell or sparks, but it needs a careful check
- **Door latch:** nuisance only, so it can wait

## Kitchen sink drip
### What to do first
- Put a dry towel under it
- Check whether the leak is from:
  - the supply line
  - the shutoff valve
  - the trap or drain
- Since you can shut off the sink valves, that’s your first clean stop if it starts getting worse

### Watch for
- water reaching the back of the cabinet
- swelling wood
- drip getting faster
- leak from above instead of below

## Back bedroom outlet
### What to check first
- Try the breaker once if you haven’t already
- Check whether another outlet on the same area is dead
- Test the lamp somewhere else so you know the lamp itself works

### Stop if
- breaker trips again
- outlet feels warm
- you see scorch marks
- anything smells burnt

### Don’t do
- don’t open the outlet box if you’re not already comfortable with that
- don’t keep resetting anything over and over

## Bathroom door latch
### Last on the list
- tighten the latch plate screws
- check if the strike plate is slightly out of line
- if it still sticks, leave it for later

## Simple rule
- **Water first**
- **Power second, but carefully**
- **Door latch last**

If you want, I can turn this into a **small do-now / check-first / leave-alone list** for the kitchen drawer.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah. That’s usable.

The order is right, and the reasons are plain enough:
- sink drip first, because water under a cabinet can turn ugly
- outlet second, but only with care
- latch last, since it’s just a nuisance

I’d probably keep it even simpler on the outlet: if the breaker trips again, or there’s any warmth or scorch mark, I’d stop there. No sense forcing it.

And the kitchen one — I’d want to know whether it’s the supply line, the valve, or the drain before I start tightening things down. A towel under it first, sure. That part’s sensible.

So yes, that’s enough to work from.
